questions.question: Explain the two shared variables used in Peterson's solution and their purposes.
keyPoints: flag[2] - Boolean array where flag[i] indicates if process i wants to enter critical section
turn - Integer variable that resolves conflicts when both processes want to enter
flag shows intention/wish, turn makes the final decision when both want in

questions.question: State and explain the three requirements of the critical section problem that Peterson's solution satisfies.
keyPoints: Mutual Exclusion - Only one process can be in critical section at any time
Progress - If no process is in critical section and some want to enter, only those not in remainder section participate in decision
Bounded Waiting - After a process indicates desire to enter, there is a limit on how many times other processes can enter before this process gets in

questions.question: What are the limitations of Peterson's solution?
keyPoints: Works only for exactly two processes
Cannot be directly extended to handle N processes
Relies on atomic load/store assumptions which may fail on modern multi-core processors with memory reordering
Uses busy-waiting (spin-lock) which wastes CPU cycles

questions.question: Why is Peterson's solution called a software-based solution?
keyPoints: Does not require any special hardware atomic instructions like test-and-set or compare-and-swap
Achieves mutual exclusion using only regular shared variables and standard load/store operations
Demonstrates that mutual exclusion can be solved purely through software coordination
The solution runs entirely in user space without kernel support

questions.question: Explain the busy-waiting (spin-lock) aspect of Peterson's solution and its disadvantages.
keyPoints: In entry section, process waits in a while loop checking conditions continuously
Disadvantage: wastes CPU cycles as process constantly checks without doing useful work
Can cause performance degradation in multi-core systems due to cache coherency traffic
Better alternatives: block/wakeup mechanisms using semaphores or condition variables

questions.question: Write the pseudocode for Peterson's solution for two processes P0 and P1, clearly showing the entry, critical, and exit sections.
keyPoints: Initialize: flag[0] = flag[1] = false, turn = 0 or 1
Process i code: do { entry section, critical section, exit section, remainder section } while (true)
Entry: flag[i] = true; turn = j; while (flag[j] == true && turn == j);
Exit: flag[i] = false;
Explain each line's purpose and how mutual exclusion is achieved
Show that both cannot be in critical section simultaneously due to flag and turn combination

questions.question: Explain with an example how Peterson's solution ensures mutual exclusion. Show why both processes cannot be in the critical section simultaneously.
keyPoints: For both to be in CS simultaneously, both flag[0] and flag[1] must be true
If both in CS, then P0 must have passed while condition: flag[1]==false OR turn==0
Similarly P1 must have passed: flag[0]==false OR turn==1
Contradiction: If both flags are true, turn must be either 0 or 1, not both
Therefore one process must wait at the while loop
The turn variable combined with flag prevents simultaneous entry

questions.question: Explain how Peterson's solution guarantees bounded waiting. Also discuss why it may not work correctly on modern multi-core processors.
keyPoints: Bounded waiting: After P0 indicates desire (sets flag[0]=true), P1 can enter at most once before P0 enters
This is because if P1 wants to enter again, it must set turn=0, allowing P0 to enter
On modern multi-core: CPU/memory reordering can cause operations to appear out of order
Without memory barriers, the order of flag setting and turn assignment may not be visible to other cores
This can break the mutual exclusion guarantee on weakly ordered architectures like ARM, PowerPC

questions.question: Compare Peterson's solution with hardware-based solutions (like test-and-set) for achieving mutual exclusion. List advantages and disadvantages of each approach.
keyPoints: Hardware solutions: Use atomic instructions (test-and-set, compare-and-swap)
Advantage of hardware: Simpler, can work for any number of processes, more efficient on modern CPUs
Disadvantage of hardware: Requires special CPU instructions, less portable
Advantage of Peterson: Pure software, no hardware support needed, educational value
Disadvantage of Peterson: Only 2 processes, busy-waiting wastes CPU, assumes atomic memory operations
Peterson's solution is primarily of historical and educational importance
